serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of salmon katsu don is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with a breaded and deep-fried salmon cutlet.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon Katsu Don — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Salmon Katsu Don (cr_id F05100120) at confidence 0.95. The HPB record describes the dish as rice with cooked salmon, without sauce, consistent with the input description.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salmon katsu don as a composite dish. The GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: white jasmine/short-grain rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The salmon and panko coating contribute negligible additional carbohydrate relative to the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 90.76) / 100 ≈ 65.3; however, using the per-100g carbohydrate of 25.93 g and a 350 g serving: carbohydrate per serving = 90.76 g; GL = (72 × 90.76) / 100 = 65.3. Reported GL rounded to 65.3 for a 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 197 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 690 mg sodium, representing 30% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request the dish without added soy sauce or dipping condiments to keep sodium intake below 400 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ≈ 72) with 25.93 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 1.25 g dietary fibre per 100 g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 91 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, portion should not exceed 150 g, brown rice substitution should be requested, and the meal should be paired with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying the salmon in oil raises total fat to 6.99 g per 100 g with an estimated saturated fat of 1.20 g per 100 g; the frying process also generates oxidised lipids and potential trans fats that are pro-atherogenic.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer grilled or baked salmon preparations over the katsu (breaded and deep-fried) version, and should limit portion size to reduce total fat and sodium load per meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon katsu don is a Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with a breaded and deep-fried salmon cutlet (katsu). The word 'don' (丼) refers to a bowl of rice with a topping, and 'katsu' denotes the panko-breaded, deep-fried preparation method. In this version, the dish is served without sauce, making it a simpler, drier preparation compared to the classic egg-simmered katsudon. The salmon fillet is coated in wheat flour, egg wash, and panko breadcrumbs before being deep-fried until golden and crisp. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior, moist and flaky salmon interior, and a neutral, starchy rice base. Nutritionally, the dish provides 182 kcal per 100 g, with 4.68 g protein, 6.99 g fat, and 25.93 g carbohydrate. The white rice base is the dominant macronutrient contributor and carries a high glycemic index of approximately 72, making the dish a high-glycemic-load meal at standard serving sizes. Dietary fibre is low at 1.25 g per 100 g. Sodium is moderate at 197 mg per 100 g but increases significantly if soy sauce or condiments are added. The dish is popular in Japanese restaurants and food courts across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to the high-GI rice base and large carbohydrate load per serving. Those with hypertension should monitor sodium intake from condiments. The deep-frying method reduces the cardiovascular benefit of salmon's natural omega-3 fatty acids and introduces oxidised lipids.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database entry for Salmon Katsu Don (cr_id F05100120) at a confidence of 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. The GI is estimated from the white rice component using a peer-reviewed published GI table (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), which is an approved GI source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable breaded and fried fish dishes as the HPB record does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific composite dish (salmon katsu don without sauce) are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite preparation as of 2026-04-16. 2. Saturated fat of 1.20 g per 100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for breaded and fried fish (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 15211) as the closest approved-source equivalent; this is an adaptation and is documented accordingly. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for salmon katsu don as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Glycemic load per serving (350 g): GL = (72 × 90.76) / 100 = 65.3; the field glycemic_load_per_serving stores 65.3 for the 350 g serving. 5. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced an estimated trans fat of ~0.1 g per 100 g; this claim is not supported by an approved source and has been excluded. Trans fat is set to -1 pending approved-source confirmation. 6. The dish is described in the input as 'rice with cooked salmon only, without sauce'; the katsu preparation implies breading and frying, which is reflected in the classification and health context. 7. No Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names are assigned as this is a Japanese dish; only Japanese is mandatory per the multilingual rules.
